steps.content: A string is a sequence of characters — letters, numbers, spaces, punctuation — wrapped in quotes. Strings are how Python handles text, and they are everywhere in programming.
You can use single quotes '...' or double quotes "...". Both work the same. Use double quotes if your text contains an apostrophe, and single quotes if it contains double quotes.
Use + to concatenate (join) strings together. Use * to repeat a string a given number of times.
The len() function returns the number of characters in a string. Python also provides many built-in string methods: upper(), lower(), strip() (removes surrounding whitespace), and replace().

text = "  Hello World  "
print(len(text))
print(text.upper())
print(text.lower())
print(text.strip())
print(text.replace("World", "Python"))
Strings are immutable — methods like upper() and strip() don't change the original string. They return a new string instead. The original stays the same.
You can access individual characters using square brackets with the index number. Python uses zero-based indexing, so [0] is the first character. Use [start:end] to slice a portion of the string.
An f-string lets you embed variables directly inside a string. Prefix the string with f and use { } to insert variable values.

name = "Alice"
age = 12
print(f"{name} is {age} years old")
f-strings are the modern, recommended way to format strings in Python 3.6+. They are cleaner and faster than older approaches like % formatting or .format().
